title: Whacking Day

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams] [Tires Screeching]
jokes.setup: Opening credits sequence showing Homer at work
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation followed by what's implied to be a car accident
jokes.explanation: Homer's iconic catchphrase in the opening sequence - highly quotable and character-specific but routine by this point in the series

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Children, we've just been tipped off that Superintendent Chalmers... is planning a surprise inspection. So, let's clean up this pigsty.
jokes.setup: Skinner addressing students about cleaning up for inspection
jokes.punchline: Calling his own school a 'pigsty' while trying to impress the superintendent
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing Skinner's neurotic honesty undermining his authority

dialogue.speaker: Seymour Skinner
dialogue.line: Now children, Superintendent Chalmers is coming to inspect our school, so I want everything spotless. This facility will not be a pigsty on my watch!

dialogue.speaker: Seymour Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, perhaps it's a small pigsty, but it's OUR pigsty.

jokes.text: I want you to keep filling your shirt with crud till I get back. Y-Yes, sir.
jokes.setup: Skinner giving bizarre instructions during cleanup
jokes.punchline: Student's obedient compliance to an obviously ridiculous request
jokes.explanation: Absurd command that highlights both Skinner's frazzled state and the students' conditioned obedience

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Now students, I want this gymnasium spotless. And I mean spotless. I want you to get down on your hands and knees and lick every square inch of this floor.

dialogue.speaker: Unnamed Student
dialogue.line: Even the parts where Ralph threw up?

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Especially those parts.

dialogue.speaker: Unnamed Student
dialogue.line: Okay, Principal Skinner.

jokes.text: Fools. [Evil Laugh] [Laughter Continues]
jokes.setup: Skinner believing he's tricked the troublemakers
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top villain laugh revealing his petty satisfaction
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Skinner's repressed authoritarian tendencies breaking through his mild exterior

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Excellent! I've finally outsmarted those troublemakers! Mwahahahahaha!

jokes.text: Oops. Still on. [Shuts Off]
jokes.setup: Skinner's evil laugh continuing
jokes.punchline: Realizing he's still on the intercom, exposing his villainous moment
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe comedy - the delayed realization and awkward correction creates perfect embarrassment

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Excellent, excellent... *evil laugh* ...now to implement my diabolical plan—

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: *pause* Oh no. The intercom.

jokes.text: Hey, what do you think he meant by that 'fools' remark? Aw, who cares? Time to get me a mountain bike.
jokes.setup: Students hearing Skinner's villainous laugh
jokes.punchline: Their casual dismissal despite the obvious warning sign
jokes.explanation: Shows the kids' typical obliviousness to obvious danger signals in favor of immediate gratification

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Did you hear that? That's the laugh of pure evil.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Nah, that's just Skinner.

jokes.text: Sorry about the ruse, gentlemen. [Grunts] You're being swept under the rug for the superintendent's visit.
jokes.setup: Students discovering they've been tricked
jokes.punchline: Skinner's literal use of 'swept under the rug' while actually hiding them
jokes.explanation: Clever literal interpretation of a common idiom that fits Skinner's pedantic character

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Wait, where's the rest of the class?

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Oh, we had some discipline issues. I swept them under the rug.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: You literally swept them under the rug?

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, yes. It seemed more efficient than the usual paperwork.

jokes.text: Would the world judge me harshly if I threw away the key? No, but the P.T.A. would tear you a new arse.
jokes.setup: Skinner contemplating abandoning the trapped students
jokes.punchline: Willie's practical concern about P.T.A. retribution being worse than moral judgment
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion - instead of moral outrage, Willie focuses on the practical bureaucratic consequences, perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Perhaps we should just leave them down there.

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Och, ye cannae do that, man! The P.T.A. will have yer head!

jokes.text: Wise counsel, William. But the potty-talk adds nothing. Aye, sir. You bath-taking, underpants-wearing lily hugger.
jokes.setup: Skinner chiding Willie for crude language
jokes.punchline: Willie's creative attempt at clean insults that are somehow more absurd
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation where trying to be clean makes the insult more ridiculous and memorable. Willie's creative profanity substitutes are hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Willie, your language is absolutely unacceptable. I want to hear you apologize using proper, civilized words.

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Aye, ye're right, Principal Skinner. I apologize for bein' such a... a great big fence post! A complete and utter... sack of haggis! Ye're nothin' but a... a two-timin' spatula with delusions of grandeur!

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well... I suppose that's... better?

jokes.text: We're dropping the geography requirement. The children weren't testing well. It was proving to be an embarrassment.
jokes.setup: Chalmers discussing curriculum changes
jokes.punchline: Solving poor test scores by eliminating the subject entirely
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of educational bureaucracy solving problems by lowering standards rather than improving teaching

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: We need to address these poor test scores. I have a solution: we're eliminating math from the curriculum entirely.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: But Superintendent, how will students learn?

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: They won't need to. Problem solved.

jokes.text: Very good. Back to the three 'R's.' Two 'R's' come October.
jokes.setup: Reference to traditional 'three R's' of education
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of further cuts coming in October
jokes.explanation: Economical escalation that extends the education decline joke with perfect timing and delivery

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, you know, Superintendent, we've always believed in the three R's: Reading, Writing, and Arithmetic.

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: That's admirable, Principal Skinner. However, I'm afraid budget cuts mean we'll have to eliminate one of them.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Which one?

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: All of them. And there are more cuts coming in October.

jokes.text: What do you think of the banners? Transparent toadying. They were the children's idea. I tried to stop them.
jokes.setup: Skinner showing off banners for the superintendent
jokes.punchline: Chalmers' blunt assessment and Skinner's immediate blame-shifting to children
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment showing Skinner's pathetic attempts at flattery backfiring spectacularly

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: And here we have our new banners celebrating academic excellence!

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: These are the saddest banners I've ever seen.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, the children made them.

jokes.text: It's always the children's fault, isn't it, Seymour? Yes. Yes, it is, sir.
jokes.setup: Chalmers making a sarcastic observation about Skinner's blame-shifting
jokes.punchline: Skinner completely missing the sarcasm and earnestly agreeing
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy where Skinner's desperation makes him miss obvious sarcasm and earnestly agree to his own criticism

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Yes, and I'm sure it's everyone else's fault but yours.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Exactly! Thank you for understanding, Superintendent.

jokes.text: Imagine-- a school out there with no bullies. Science geeks not getting beat up. Kids using their lunch money for food. I can't take it! [Grunts]
jokes.setup: Chalmers describing an ideal school environment
jokes.punchline: His horror at the concept of a peaceful, functional school
jokes.explanation: Dark subversion where the education administrator is horrified by the idea of actual educational success

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: A school where students actually learn, teachers are paid fairly, and there are no budget crises... *voice trembling* It's too much. Too perfect. It can't exist.

jokes.text: May I interest you in a Jell-O brick, sir? There's a grape in the center. Well, I'm not made of stone.
jokes.setup: Offering an unappetizing institutional food item
jokes.punchline: Chalmers' acceptance based on the 'not made of stone' phrase fitting literally with the brick description
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay connecting the literal 'brick' description with the idiom about being made of stone

dialogue.speaker: Lunch Lady Doris
dialogue.line: It's not made of stone.

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Well, that's good enough for me!

jokes.text: You're not made of stone. [Chuckles] A brick and you're not made of... stone.
jokes.setup: Chalmers realizing the wordplay he just made
jokes.punchline: His awkward attempt to highlight and explain his own weak joke
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about explaining weak jokes, creating cringe comedy through Chalmers' self-satisfaction

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: I was just making a little wordplay there. Did you catch that? It was rather clever, I thought.

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: You see, I said 'principal' but the way I inflected it suggested 'principle' — two entirely different meanings, yet phonetically similar. Quite droll, really.

jokes.text: Were it not a violation of God's law, I'd make you my wife. [Loud Kiss] Now thar's a lonely man.
jokes.setup: Willie expressing affection for his tractor
jokes.punchline: His dramatic romantic declaration to farm equipment, observed by an equally lonely drunk
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Willie - his passionate nature applied to farm equipment, with Barney's observation adding the perfect capper

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Oh, me beloved tractor, ye are the love of me life. Without ye, I am but a shell of a man.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: I know what ya mean, Willie. I got that same feelin' about me bottle of whiskey.

jokes.text: I'm new in town. Be there a cool loch... where a lass could wash her long, red hair? Nay, but there's a pool at me apartment complex. There was a rat in the deep end. But we got 'im!
jokes.setup: Romantic inquiry about a scenic location
jokes.punchline: Willie's practical but unsexy alternative, made worse by the rat detail delivered with pride
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from romantic to mundane to gross, with Willie's enthusiastic delivery making it worse

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: This is such a beautiful spot. Do you come here often?

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Aye, I come here to dump my garden waste. Found a dead rat here once—a real beauty, ten pounder!

jokes.text: Well, let's, uh, pick one at random. Mmm, how about that one? You mean this boy here? No! No. Lisa Simpson.
jokes.setup: Chalmers wanting to test a random student
jokes.punchline: Skinner's obvious steering away from a problem student toward his star pupil
jokes.explanation: Classic Skinner manipulation showing his desperation to impress the superintendent through deception

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: I'd like to test a random student.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Of course. Bart, come up here.

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: I said random.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: I am choosing randomly.

jokes.text: When was the Battle of New Orleans? January 8, 1815. Two weeks after the war ended.
jokes.setup: Standard history question and correct answer
jokes.punchline: Lisa's additional fact revealing the battle's historical irony
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment - not only knows the answer but provides additional context that reveals historical irony

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Who won the Battle of Hastings?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: William the Conqueror in 1066.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Interestingly, despite being called 'the Conqueror,' William actually had a legitimate claim to the English throne through his great-aunt Emma of Normandy, making the Norman Conquest less of an invasion and more of a dynastic succession dispute.

jokes.text: What's a battle? [Forced Laughter] Let's go.
jokes.setup: Ralph's delayed, confused question
jokes.punchline: His complete lack of understanding of basic concepts, and Skinner's panicked damage control
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph moment showing his characteristic confusion about basic concepts, creating maximum embarrassment for Skinner

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Why do we need a king, if we have a president?

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, uh, that's a good question, Ralph. You see, the king and the president are... they're different things.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: My dad says the king is a bad man.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: No, no, Ralph. The king is not a bad man. He's just, uh... well, he's a different kind of leader.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Is the president also a bad man?

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: No! I mean... that's not really the point here.

jokes.text: Did that boy say, 'What's a battle?' He said, 'What's that rattle?' About the heating duct.
jokes.setup: Chalmers questioning what he heard
jokes.punchline: Skinner's desperate improvised explanation
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe comedy as Skinner desperately tries to cover up Ralph's embarrassing question with an obvious lie

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Skinner, what in blazes was that noise?

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Uh, that would be the... uh... the new automated lunch system. Yes. Very efficient. Saves money.

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: It sounded like an explosion.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, yes, small explosions are part of the... startup sequence. Perfectly normal. Happens all the time.

jokes.text: Oh, so you would hear 'R's' as 'B's'? Yes. I understand.
jokes.setup: Chalmers testing Skinner's cold excuse
jokes.punchline: Logical inconsistency in Skinner's excuse being politely exposed
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation where Chalmers politely destroys Skinner's lie through simple logic

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: You say you have a cold, Principal Skinner, yet you were just shouting quite vigorously in the hallway.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, I... the cold affects different people in different ways.

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: I see. And the energetic calisthenics I observed you performing this morning?

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Exercise helps fight infection, Superintendent.

jokes.text: Come on, Bart. Ride me. I better not. [Imitating Chicken] He's insulting both of us.
jokes.setup: Nelson trying to get Bart to ride him like a horse
jokes.punchline: Chicken sounds used as insult leading to violence
jokes.explanation: Typical bully dynamics with the absurdity of being insulted by chicken sounds escalating to violence

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Come on, Simpson. Ride me like a horse.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Bawk bawk bawk bawk!

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Why you little...

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha!

jokes.text: Excellent. Not a trace of urine. Seymour, you run a tight ship.
jokes.setup: Chalmers inspecting school facilities
jokes.punchline: Setting 'no urine' as the standard for educational excellence
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion where the absence of urine is treated as a major accomplishment in school management

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Well, I must say, Principal Skinner, setting 'no urine' as the standard for educational excellence is a bit of a low bar, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Superintendent Chalmers, I assure you we've made tremendous progress. Why, just last week we went three whole days without an incident.

jokes.text: Well, you know what they say, sir. 'Where there's no smoke, there's no fire.' What an odd remark.
jokes.setup: Skinner attempting to use a common saying
jokes.punchline: Getting the idiom completely backwards, and Chalmers' understated response
jokes.explanation: Classic Skinner moment where his nervous attempts at wisdom result in mangled idioms

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, Seymour, I always say you have to put all your eggs in one basket and watch that basket like a hawk!

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: That's... not how that saying goes.

jokes.text: Why, that looks like a 50-cent piece. I'll just bend over and get it. [Yelling] Seems to be caught... between these two flowers. [More Yelling]
jokes.setup: Chalmers spotting money on the ground
jokes.punchline: The trapped kids using the opportunity to attack him while he's vulnerable
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy escalation where the kids take advantage of Chalmers' vulnerable position

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Money! I must pick it up!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Now!

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Get him!

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! Why are you children attacking me?

jokes.text: Seymour, I was thinking of promoting you to assistant superintendent. But, instead, that plum goes to Holloway. No. But, sir, he's a drunk. And a pill-popper.
jokes.setup: Chalmers mentioning a promotion opportunity
jokes.punchline: The revelation that the job goes to someone with obvious substance abuse problems
jokes.explanation: Dark satire of bureaucratic incompetence where merit is inversely related to advancement

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Principal Skinner, I'm pleased to inform you that the superintendent position has opened up. You're my top candidate.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, thank you, Superintendent. I'm honored.

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: However, the board has decided to give the job to Barney Gumble.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Barney? But he's a hopeless drunk!

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Yes, well, the board feels his unique perspective will bring fresh ideas to the district.

jokes.text: And why is a cafeteria worker posing as a nurse? I get two paychecks this way. D'oh.
jokes.setup: Chalmers discovering the school nurse is actually the lunch lady
jokes.punchline: Her matter-of-fact admission of fraud for financial gain
jokes.explanation: Great setup-punchline with the unexpected honesty about corruption, followed by Homer's perfect 'D'oh' reaction

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: You're the school nurse? But you're also the lunch lady!

dialogue.speaker: Lunch Lady Doris
dialogue.line: That's right. One job wasn't paying enough, so I figured I'd double up. Who's gonna know?

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: This is fraud!

dialogue.speaker: Lunch Lady Doris
dialogue.line: Call it what you want. I call it making ends meet.

jokes.text: There's no detention this time, Simpson. This is the end. You are expelled from Springfield Elementary.
jokes.setup: Skinner's mounting frustration with Bart
jokes.punchline: The extreme overreaction of expulsion for the current incident
jokes.explanation: Skinner's characteristic overreaction and dramatic pronouncement, setting up the episode's main plot

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: That's it, Simpson! You're expelled!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Expelled?! For what?!

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: For existing in my school!

jokes.text: [Laughing] [Maniacal Laughter] Aw.
jokes.setup: Bart initially enjoying his expulsion
jokes.punchline: Quick realization that this might actually be bad news
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character beat - initial delight at rebellion followed by dawning realization of consequences

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba! I'm expelled! This is the greatest day of my life!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... my mom's gonna kill me.

jokes.text: And we'll visit a nudist camp... for animals.
jokes.setup: Standard TV news tease format
jokes.punchline: The absurd concept of animals needing a nudist camp when they're naturally naked
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist concept that highlights the logical inconsistency through deadpan news delivery

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: Coming up next: A controversial new camp for animals who want to get back to nature. But these creatures are already naked! We'll tell you why some say this nudist colony for beasts is just plain ridiculous.

jokes.text: But, first, a look at the local holiday that was called... distasteful and puerile by a panel of hillbillies, 'Whacking Day.'
jokes.setup: Building up negative criticism of the holiday
jokes.punchline: The critics being 'hillbillies' - implying the holiday is so bad even they find it distasteful
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation joke - if even hillbillies find something distasteful, it must be truly awful

dialogue.speaker: TV Announcer
dialogue.line: A holiday so bad, even hillbillies hate it.

jokes.text: I-Is Whacking Day over? [Jeering] Thank you. Thanks for coming out.
jokes.setup: Nixon as grand marshal failing at snake whacking
jokes.punchline: His pathetic attempt to gracefully exit after public humiliation
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe comedy with Nixon's characteristic awkwardness in trying to maintain dignity after failure

dialogue.speaker: Nixon
dialogue.line: I am not a crook!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Boo!

dialogue.speaker: Nixon
dialogue.line: Well, I'm outta here.

jokes.text: I learned how many drams in a pennyweight. I got expelled. That's my boy. [Gulping] Mmm, beer. What?
jokes.setup: Kids reporting their school experiences
jokes.punchline: Homer's delayed, beer-delayed realization that Bart got expelled
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - initial pride in Bart's rebellion, then beer-delayed shock at the reality

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, Bart got expelled from school today.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... beer.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, did you hear what Lisa said?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wha—? Expelled? Why didn't anyone tell me?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: We just did.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, I wasn't listening. Now where's my beer?

jokes.text: No son of mine is gonna be a 19th-century cockney bootblack.
jokes.setup: Bart suggesting he'll work as a bootblack
jokes.punchline: Homer's weirdly specific objection to the historical and cultural aspects rather than the child labor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - objecting to historically and culturally specific aspects while missing the actual problem of child labor

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I could be a bootblack!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A bootblack? That's a racial slur!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No, Dad, it's a guy who shines boots.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's still not okay. Back in my day, we had standards. A bootblack wears a little hat, does a little dance... it's demeaning to the entire bootblack community.

jokes.text: Now, Bart, Since you're new here... perhaps you'd favor us with a psalm. How about 'Beans, beans, the musical fruit'?
jokes.setup: Religious teacher asking for a psalm
jokes.punchline: Bart offering the crude children's rhyme instead
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart inappropriate response to authority, with the religious setting making it worse

dialogue.speaker: Religious School Teacher
dialogue.line: Can anyone recite a psalm?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Psalm? I know one! Rub-a-dub-dub, thanks for the grub, yeah!

jokes.text: Well, uh, beans were a staple of the Israelites. Yes, proceed.
jokes.setup: Teacher trying to find religious relevance in Bart's crude song
jokes.punchline: Desperately rationalizing beans as biblical to avoid confrontation
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation where the teacher's desperation to find religious meaning makes everything worse

dialogue.speaker: Religious School Teacher
dialogue.line: Well, beans are mentioned in the Bible! Remember when Esau sold his birthright for a bowl of... well, it was probably beans. Yes, beans!

jokes.text: Avert your eyes, children. He may take on other forms.
jokes.setup: Bart being ejected from religious school
jokes.punchline: Teacher treating Bart like a supernatural threat requiring protective measures
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation treating Bart's misbehavior as demonic possession, perfectly economical and quotable

dialogue.speaker: Religious School Teacher
dialogue.line: We've tried everything - holy water, exorcism, even playing Christian rock backwards.

dialogue.speaker: Religious School Teacher
dialogue.line: The only solution is to expel him immediately and perform a blessing on the entire classroom.

jokes.text: We'd like you to try this new diet cola. We call it 'Nature's Goodness.' What's in it? 2-4 desoxypropenuramine.
jokes.setup: Product testing with a natural-sounding name
jokes.punchline: The completely unnatural, chemical-sounding ingredient name
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of deceptive marketing - 'Nature's Goodness' containing completely artificial chemicals

jokes.text: Sweet. Pleasing taste. Some 'monsterism.'
jokes.setup: Bart taste-testing the experimental cola
jokes.punchline: Casually reporting supernatural transformation as a minor side effect
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery treating monstrous transformation as a mild inconvenience, brilliant economy

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, this stuff is great! It's like licking a battery, but in a good way.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Although, uh, I think I'm starting to levitate a little bit here.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: My skin is turning purple, but that's cool. I'm down with it.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh, and apparently I can see through walls now. Pretty sweet side effect if you ask me.

jokes.text: The boy's 10 years old. Let's focus our energy on Lisa and the other one. What's your problem?
jokes.setup: Homer writing off Bart's education
jokes.punchline: Referring to his own baby daughter as 'the other one' and her aggressive response
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer thoughtlessness combined with Maggie's characteristic violent response to being ignored

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I guess I can write off Bart's education. At least I still have the other one.

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: *sucks pacifier aggressively*

jokes.text: Inside every man is a struggle between good and evil that cannot be resolved. [Singsongy] I am evil Homer. I am evil Homer.
jokes.setup: Homer philosophizing about human nature
jokes.punchline: Immediately embodying the evil side in the most childish way possible
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - attempting philosophy then immediately undermining it with childish behavior

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, there's a good side to human nature and a bad side.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna be bad! D'oh!

jokes.text: [Chuckling] I bought a bell.
jokes.setup: Marge setting up home schooling
jokes.punchline: Her simple pride in acquiring the most basic school equipment
jokes.explanation: Sweet Marge moment showing her earnest attempts at creating a proper school environment

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I've set up a little classroom right here in the living room. I got a desk, a chalkboard, and even a globe!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'm so proud of myself. Look at all this school equipment. We're really doing this!

jokes.text: [Ringing Shrilly] [Both Scream] I think I'll unplug that.
jokes.setup: Marge proudly using her school bell
jokes.punchline: The bell being painfully loud in a home environment
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy showing Marge's inexperience with actual teaching equipment

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Look how beautifully this school bell rings!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mom, that's deafening!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Nonsense, it's melodious.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: My ears are bleeding!

jokes.text: I got separated from my platoon after we parachuted into Düsseldorf. So I rode out the war posing as a German cabaret singer.
jokes.setup: Grandpa telling a war story as guest speaker
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous survival strategy of cross-dressing as entertainment
jokes.explanation: Classic Grandpa tall tale with the perfect mix of historical detail and absurd personal survival story

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: So there I was, surrounded by enemy troops, no way out. I knew I had to think fast. So I put on a dress, high heels, the whole nine yards. Started doing a little song and dance number.

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, those fellas laughed so hard, they forgot all about shooting me! Turns out a well-timed musical number is the best defense against enemy fire.

jokes.text: Ach du Liebe! Das ist not eine booby.
jokes.setup: Grandpa performing in his cabaret singer persona
jokes.punchline: His terrible German accent and inappropriate lyrics
jokes.explanation: Cringe comedy from Grandpa's inappropriate performance, though relies somewhat on crude humor

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: Velcome, velcome to my cabaret! I vill now sing for you ze traditional German love songs!

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: ♪ Ohhh, Maggie vith ze yellow hair, I vish to touch your... Medicare forms! ♪

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: ♪ And Marge, your blue beehive, it reminds me of ze time I forgot vhere I parked my car! ♪

jokes.text: Is that story true, Grampa? Well, most of it. I did wear a dress for a period in the '40s.
jokes.setup: Bart questioning the truthfulness of the war story
jokes.punchline: Grandpa admitting the cross-dressing part was real but for unstated reasons
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - the most unbelievable part of the story turns out to be true, leaving the real reason mysterious

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Grandpa, is any of this stuff true?

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa
dialogue.line: Well, the cross-dressing part was real.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Why'd you do that?

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa
dialogue.line: I don't wanna talk about it.

jokes.text: Look what I got, Marge. A new whacking stick. Whack! Ooh. Excuse me. Whack, whack, whack! Whoa, whoa, whoa!
jokes.setup: Homer showing off his snake-whacking stick
jokes.punchline: Accidentally hitting Marge and then continuing to demonstrate despite her protests
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer physical comedy showing his obliviousness to causing harm while pursuing his interests

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Behold! My snake-whacking stick!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Watch as I demonstrate its deadly power!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, be careful—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hyah!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Ow! Homer, you hit me!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That was just a practice swing. Now for the real demonstration!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, stop! You're going to hurt someone!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, please. I know what I'm doing. Hyah! Hyah!

jokes.text: It's about a boy who goes to war. His hand is deformed in an accident. Deformed? Why didn't you say so? They should call this book 'Johnny Deformed.'
jokes.setup: Marge trying to interest Bart in classic literature
jokes.punchline: Bart's immediate interest in the gruesome aspects and his crude retitling
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character moment - only interested in violence and gore, immediately wanting to rename the classic

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, I'd like you to read this classic novel. It's considered one of the greatest works of literature.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. Does it have any cool murders or gross stuff?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but that's not really the point. The author explores themes of morality and—

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba! I'm gonna call it 'The Decapitation Station.'

jokes.text: Just squeeze your rage into a bitter little ball... and release it at an appropriate time. Like that day I hit the referee with a whiskey bottle. Remember that?
jokes.setup: Homer giving Lisa advice about managing emotions
jokes.punchline: His example being violent assault with a weapon, treated as fond nostalgia
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer parenting - giving psychologically harmful advice while fondly reminiscing about violence

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, when you get sad, you run outside and play in the snow, cry your eyes out, then drink hot chocolate till you throw up.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That doesn't sound very healthy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, it worked for me. Why, I remember one time I was really upset, so I just took a tire iron and...

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: ...and smashed up Barney's car! Those were the days.

jokes.text: Yes. I played Panicky Idiot Number Two in The Poseidon Adventure. Sorry, we're looking for more of a 'duh, duh' idiot.
jokes.setup: Barney describing his acting experience
jokes.punchline: Being rejected for not being stupid enough despite playing an idiot
jokes.explanation: Great escalation joke - even Barney's professional idiot credentials aren't sufficient for the required stupidity level

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: I went to an audition for a commercial. The casting director said, 'We need someone stupid.' I said, 'I can do that!' But then they said I wasn't stupid enough.

dialogue.speaker: Casting Director
dialogue.line: You're too smart to play an idiot.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: But I AM an idiot!

dialogue.speaker: Casting Director
dialogue.line: An idiot wouldn't know he's an idiot.

jokes.text: Actually, I'm supposed to be a governor. [Burps]
jokes.setup: Barney being asked if he's playing the town drunk
jokes.punchline: Revealing he's cast as a government official while being obviously intoxicated
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony with Barney cast against type as an authority figure while exemplifying his actual character

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Playing? I'm not playing anything. I'm a government official.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: See? *hiccup* ...official.

jokes.text: The enemy surrounded the fort and said that if the captain was sent out... the rest would be spared. What did they do? They sent him out. Was he killed? And how. That's why they call it Fort Sensible.
jokes.setup: Historical story about military sacrifice
jokes.punchline: The supposedly honorable fort being named for cowardly pragmatism
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of heroic historical narratives - the 'sensible' choice was cowardly betrayal

dialogue.speaker: Historical Guide
dialogue.line: This fort was named after a great military hero who died defending it from the British.

dialogue.speaker: Tourist
dialogue.line: What an honor. What was his name?

dialogue.speaker: Historical Guide
dialogue.line: Fort Prudence.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Prudence? That's not a hero's name!

dialogue.speaker: Historical Guide
dialogue.line: Well, the actual hero died, so we named it after the guy who retreated.

jokes.text: Hey, wait a minute. That was the same day he was at Ticonderoga. How could he be in two places at once?
jokes.setup: Historical presentation showing founder in different locations
jokes.punchline: Bart catching the logical impossibility that exposes the lie
jokes.explanation: Good setup for exposing historical fraud, with Bart's academic skills ironically helping him catch lies

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. If the founder was in two places at the same time, that's impossible. He's totally making this up!

jokes.text: He'll blow the whole deal. Get him out of here. Roger.
jokes.setup: Bart discovering the historical inconsistency
jokes.punchline: The immediate conspiracy to silence a child asking logical questions
jokes.explanation: Escalation showing how invested the town is in maintaining historical lies, even against children

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, if Lincoln was president in 1865, how could he have fought in the Civil War that started in 1861?

dialogue.speaker: Historical Guide
dialogue.line: Well, young man, that's a very astute observation. Perhaps we should move along to the next exhibit.

dialogue.speaker: Security
dialogue.line: Yeah, let's go, kid. Now.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But I'm just asking a simple question about—

dialogue.speaker: Security
dialogue.line: We heard what you asked. Come on.

jokes.text: I've hidden a snake somewhere in the store. The first one to whack it, gets a free Squishy. [People Yelling] I should've put more thought into my promotion.
jokes.setup: Apu's promotional contest involving finding a hidden snake
jokes.punchline: The immediate chaos and his regret about the poorly planned promotion
jokes.explanation: Great escalation with Apu's characteristic business enthusiasm leading to immediate disaster and self-awareness

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: I have hidden a rare cobra somewhere in the Kwik-E-Mart! Find it and win a year's supply of free Squishees!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: FIND THE SNAKE! FIND THE SNAKE!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Wait, wait! I have made a terrible mistake!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: THE SNAKE! THE SNAKE!

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: No, no, no! Everybody please remain calm! The snake is very docile and—

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: AHHHHHHH!

jokes.text: And the Lord said, 'Whackye all the serpents which crawl on their bellies... and thy town shall be a beacon unto others.' Let me see that.
jokes.setup: Lovejoy reading supposedly biblical justification for Whacking Day
jokes.punchline: Lisa's immediate skepticism and demand to verify the source
jokes.explanation: Sets up the religious fraud angle with Lisa's characteristic fact-checking challenging authority

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Here it is, in the Bible: Whacking Day, April 10th. 'And the Lord said, "Thou shalt smite the snakes."'

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Reverend Lovejoy, that's not in the Bible.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: It's not? Well, I'm sure it will be once we all agree on it.

jokes.text: What's Whacking Day? Did you bring the pre-whacked snakes? Beautiful.
jokes.setup: Innocent question about the holiday
jokes.punchline: Revelation that the snakes are killed in advance for convenience
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about commercialization - even violence becomes pre-packaged convenience

dialogue.speaker: Guest
dialogue.line: So when do you kill the snakes for the holiday?

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: Oh, we kill them all in advance for convenience.

jokes.text: Well, Marge, should I whack slow or fast? Slow, then fast. [Both Laugh]
jokes.setup: Homer asking about technique
jokes.punchline: Marge's enthusiastic participation despite usually being the moral center
jokes.explanation: Character subversion showing even moral Marge getting caught up in the mob mentality

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So, uh, Marge, how exactly do you do that thing with your... you know?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh Homer, I'd be happy to show you!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Really? But I thought you always said that was... inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, usually it is, but for you, I'll make an exception!

jokes.text: Lisa, maybe if I'm part of that mob, I can help steer it in wise directions. Now, where's my giant foam cowboy hat and air horn?
jokes.setup: Homer rationalizing his participation as noble guidance
jokes.punchline: Immediately asking for the most ridiculous mob accessories
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer hypocrisy - noble rationalization immediately undermined by seeking the most absurd mob gear

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not joining the mob for crime—I'm joining to be a mentor figure, a guiding light for these young criminals.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Now, do you have any pinky rings in a size 10? And I'm gonna need a tommy gun, a fedora, and whatever that thing is that wise guys snap when they're being intimidating.

jokes.text: here's our grand marshal, the Prophet of Love, Larry White. Barry White. No. It says here Larry White. I know my own name. Yeah? Well, we'll see.
jokes.setup: Celebrity being introduced with wrong name
jokes.punchline: Bureaucratic insistence that the paperwork is more accurate than the person's own identity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant bureaucratic absurdity - trusting paperwork over the actual person, with the threatening 'we'll see'

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, please welcome... Barry White!

dialogue.speaker: Barry White
dialogue.line: Actually, my name is not Barry White.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: I'm sorry sir, but according to the paperwork here, you are definitely Barry White.

dialogue.speaker: Barry White
dialogue.line: No, I assure you, I know my own name.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Well the paperwork says otherwise, and the paperwork doesn't lie.

jokes.text: It's truly an honor to be here at this-- Hey, what is this all about anyway? Oh, God, no. You people make me sick.
jokes.setup: Barry White giving standard celebrity appearance speech
jokes.punchline: His horror upon learning what Whacking Day actually involves
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing a celebrity's genuine disgust contrasted with the crowd's enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Barry White
dialogue.line: Thank you, Springfield. It's a privilege to be here for this great American tradition, Whacking Day.

dialogue.speaker: Barry White
dialogue.line: Now, what exactly is Whacking Day?

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, it's when we all get together and whack snakes!

dialogue.speaker: Barry White
dialogue.line: Whack... snakes?

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Yeah! With bats and clubs!

dialogue.speaker: Barry White
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: [Cheering] Were they even listening to me? I, uh, don't think so.
jokes.setup: Barry White expressing moral outrage
jokes.punchline: The crowd cheering his condemnation as if it were support, Lisa's honest assessment
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation about mob mentality - cheering without listening to content, Lisa's dry honesty

dialogue.speaker: Barry White
dialogue.line: This is an outrage! A travesty! A blight upon our fair city!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Yaaaay! Woohoo!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: They think he's supporting it.

jokes.text: those snakes will be in here like Oprah on a baked ham.
jokes.setup: Lisa explaining how bass vibrations will attract snakes
jokes.punchline: Her unexpectedly crude simile comparing snakes to Oprah's appetite
jokes.explanation: Unexpected crudeness from normally refined Lisa, very quotable but somewhat cheap pop culture reference

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The bass vibrations will attract snakes.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: They'll come slithering in like Oprah at a all-you-can-eat buffet.

jokes.text: People of Springfield, Whacking Day is a sham. It was started in 1924, as an excuse to beat up the Irish.
jokes.setup: Lisa exposing the truth about Whacking Day
jokes.punchline: The holiday's actual origin as anti-Irish violence
jokes.explanation: Sharp historical satire revealing how tradition can mask bigotry, perfectly delivered by Lisa as truth-teller

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Whacking Day isn't about snakes at all! It's actually a cover for anti-Irish violence!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Well, I... that is to say... we prefer to think of it as a cherished Springfield tradition.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: A tradition based on beating Irish people with sticks!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Now, now, Lisa. That was a long time ago. We've evolved since then.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Have we? Because the holiday is literally next week!

jokes.text: 'Tis true. I took many a lump. But 'twas all in good fun.
jokes.setup: Lisa revealing anti-Irish origins of Whacking Day
jokes.punchline: Willie's cheerful acceptance of having been a victim of ethnic violence
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy with Willie's characteristically cheerful acceptance of past persecution

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mr. Willie, I have some terrible news. Whacking Day was actually created as an excuse to beat up Irish people.

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Oh, well, that explains a lot!

jokes.text: I'm an old man. I hate everything but Matlock. Ooh, it's on now.
jokes.setup: Old man declaring his general hatred
jokes.punchline: The one exception being Matlock, which immediately distracts him from the speech
jokes.explanation: Perfect old man character moment - curmudgeonly except for one TV obsession that immediately derails his attention

dialogue.speaker: Old Man
dialogue.line: I hate everything! I hate kids, I hate dogs, I hate nature, I hate—

dialogue.speaker: Old Man
dialogue.line: Wait, is that Matlock?

dialogue.speaker: Old Man
dialogue.line: I love Matlock.

jokes.text: I love the sexy slither of a lady snake. Oh, baby.
jokes.setup: Barry White being asked about his opinion of snakes
jokes.punchline: His characteristic sensual delivery applied to reptiles
jokes.explanation: Perfect Barry White parody applying his signature sensual style to snakes, completely in character

dialogue.speaker: Barry White
dialogue.line: Snakes... they're smooth, they're sleek, they move with a certain... confidence. Like a whisper in the dark, baby. Those scales, mmm, they shimmer like silk under moonlight. And that tongue... flickering in and out... tasting the air with such sensuality.

jokes.text: Look at this everyone. Twelve dead snakes. [All Booing] I'm sick of you people. You're nothing but a pack of fickle mush-heads.
jokes.setup: Quimby proudly displaying his snake kills
jokes.punchline: The crowd turning on him and his accurate assessment of their fickleness
jokes.explanation: Great escalation showing mob fickleness and Quimby's rare moment of accurate political analysis

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Behold! I have slain these serpents with my bare hands!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Huzzah! Bravo, Mayor Quimby!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: You know, you people are fickle.

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Boo! Boooo!

jokes.text: He's right. Give us hell, Quimby. [Crowd Cheering]
jokes.setup: Quimby insulting the crowd as fickle
jokes.punchline: The crowd immediately proving his point by cheering his insult to them
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - the crowd immediately proves Quimby's insult correct by cheering it, perfect mob psychology satire

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: You people are fickle!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Booo!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: See what I mean?

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Yaaaaay!

jokes.text: Um, anyway, as a reward for your studying... I've decided to welcome you back to our school. You'll be reunited with your chums, Nelson, Jimbo, D-- Oh, dear God!
jokes.setup: Skinner offering to readmit Bart to school
jokes.punchline: His horror upon remembering the bullies are still trapped in the basement
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the opening plot thread with perfect escalation as Skinner realizes his mistake

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Bart, I'm prepared to readmit you to Springfield Elementary.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Really? That's great, Mr. Skinner!

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Yes, well, we've all learned valuable lessons here. The bullies, however, are still trapped in the basement.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Oh God, the bullies are still down there!

jokes.text: I guess I've always used violence as a way of getting attention. Yes. Yes. Me too.
jokes.setup: Nelson having a moment of self-awareness about his bullying
jokes.punchline: Skinner's immediate identification with using violence for attention
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment where Skinner reveals his own psychological issues by relating to the bully

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Maybe I bully kids because I need attention and don't know how to get it any other way.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, I use violence to get attention too.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: No, I use it to maintain order. But I see your point.

jokes.text: Faster, Willie, faster. Now, we give 'em the bikes, no one sues. What if they're dead, sir? Then we ride these bikes to Mexico and freedom, Willie! Freedom!
jokes.setup: Skinner rushing to deliver the promised bikes
jokes.punchline: His elaborate escape plan if the kids are dead, complete with dramatic delivery
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation with Skinner's dramatic planning for the worst-case scenario, delivered with theatrical flair

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: If those children are dead, I have an escape plan. First, I'll destroy all evidence of this school's existence. Then I'll assume a new identity in Buenos Aires. I've had papers ready for years.

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Och, that's a bonnie plan, Skinner!

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Thank you, Willie. I've thought this through very carefully.

jokes.text: Freedom. [Chuckles] I'll turn you in at the first tollbooth.
jokes.setup: Willie seeming to go along with Skinner's escape plan
jokes.punchline: His immediate betrayal and practical approach to law enforcement
jokes.explanation: Perfect capper with Willie's matter-of-fact betrayal and the mundane 'tollbooth' detail making it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Aye, I'll help ye escape, Principal Skinner.

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: But first, I'm callin' the police.

tmdb_overview: Lisa takes a stand against Springfield's annual snake-bashing festival.
insight: Snake-centered episode escalates absurdist chaos to 87 points, Season 4's funniest half-hour.
